title: How to AI-Proof Your Firm's Tech Stack
description: A practical breakdown of what makes software survive the AI agent era. Using two real accounting software examples — Aiwyn and Double — CPA Jason Staats explains the key criteria for evaluating your current tools: do they offer MCP server support or at minimum a solid API? Firms that understand connectivity as the core requirement will pick tools that work with agents, not against them.

modules.title: Evaluating Your Current Tools for the Agent Era
modules.description: Learn the connectivity criteria that separates future-proof software from tools living on borrowed time.

steps.title: Understand the New Software Landscape
steps.content:
Two types of software will thrive in the AI agent era:

1. **Software that builds AI agents** — the infrastructure layer
2. **Software built for AI agents** — tools that agents can use programmatically

Most software your firm uses today falls into neither category. This is the gap you need to address proactively before being forced to.

steps.title: Learn from Aiwyn (Column Tax)
steps.content:
Aiwyn acquired Column Tax, which has filed over 1 million tax returns. They then released an **MCP server** that connects their tax engine directly to Claude.

This means tax professionals can prepare returns inside Claude — backed by a real tax engine, not an LLM guessing at tax law.

**Key question they asked:** "What if the interface was simply the agent?" That question is the one your firm should be asking about every tool you use.

steps.title: Learn from Double (formerly Keeper)
steps.content:
Double is a month-end close automation app. They shipped **MCP server support**, letting Claude connect to Double with fine-grained read/write permissions.

Their users did not want to abandon Double — they wanted agents to be able to use it too. Double made both possible.

**Takeaway:** The goal is not to replace apps with AI. It is to extend apps so that agents can participate in workflows alongside humans.

steps.title: Apply the Connectivity Test to Your Stack
steps.content:
For every tool your firm uses, ask:

**Can an AI agent push or pull information from this tool?**

- **Best case:** The tool has an MCP server
- **Acceptable:** The tool has a well-documented REST API
- **Red flag:** Closed system with no integration path

Tools that fail this test are at risk — not because they are bad tools, but because they cannot participate in automated workflows.

steps.title: Make Your Evaluation Decisions
steps.content:
After running the connectivity test on your stack:

- **Keep and invest:** Tools with MCP support or strong APIs — learn how to connect them to your workflows
- **Watch and wait:** Tools with APIs but no MCP yet — most will add it; allow 6-12 months
- **Plan to replace:** Tools with no integration path — they will slow your automation and become liabilities

Start with the tools your team uses most: practice management, document storage, communication platforms.
